user_query:
Build a cross-platform command-line tool called "projctl" that manages small project scaffolds and settings. Read the functional requirements in input/spec.md, default configuration in input/config_defaults.json, sample project data in input/sample_projects.json, and README style guidance in input/README_style.md before you start.

Deliverables under output/:
- output/projctl/ containing a complete CLI codebase in a programming language of your choice, with packaging metadata that exposes a console entry point named "projctl" (so users can install and run it as a command). Include a clear project structure (e.g., src/ with the main CLI entry) and do not hardcode any absolute paths.
- Implement commands described in input/spec.md, including at minimum: init, list (supports --json and --quiet, and can read from a file path or from standard input when appropriate), config get/set (with well-documented precedence rules), process (accepts input via stdin if no file is specified), completion (outputs a completion script), and standard options for help and version.
- Provide README.md at output/projctl/ that follows input/README_style.md. It must document: Installation, Usage, Examples (including using pipelines/stdin), Configuration (with precedence order and locations), JSON output mode and quiet mode, and Exit codes. Include an example that demonstrates running the list command with input/sample_projects.json.
- Provide basic, string-based tests under output/projctl/tests/ that validate at least that help and version text are present (these can be simple text assertions without executing external services). Also include a brief CONTRIBUTING or USAGE notes section if not already covered in README.
- Ensure the tool respects common CLI conventions: predictable subcommands and flags, helpful error messages with non-zero exit codes on failure, and output suitable for pipes and redirection.

Use only relative workspace paths in code and docs (e.g., input/ and output/). Write all files to output/projctl/ and include any supporting scripts there. Do not use environment-specific absolute paths or machine-dependent locations anywhere.
